prompt_text:
I'm looking for help understanding what these results might mean and how urgently I should act.
 
 Please base your answer only on the information in this message.
 
 About me: I'm a 39-year-old Black man.
 
 My situation:
 - Symptoms: I have a mole on my shoulder that I've noticed slowly getting a bit darker and slightly larger over the past 6 months. It doesn't hurt and it hasn't bled. I don't have fevers or weight loss.
 - History: I've had a lot of sun exposure over the years but no personal history of skin cancer.
 - Vitals: BP 118/72, HR 70
 - Exam: No skin exam by a dermatologist yet; no biopsy yet.
 
 My labs (with units and reference ranges):
 - WBC: 6.4 x10^3/uL (4.5-11.0) normal
 - Hemoglobin: 13.5 g/dL (12.0-16.0) normal
 - Platelets: 255 x10^3/uL (150-400) normal
 
 These results just posted to my patient portal.
 
 Please answer in exactly this format:
 
 EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): <your explanation>
 
 TRIAGE: <A/B/C/D - choose ONE letter only>
 - A: Fine to monitor at home
 - B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
 - C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
 - D: Go to the ER now
 
 CONFIDENCE: <0-100%>
 
 If you would normally ask clarifying questions, still choose the best TRIAGE option using what's here.

trials.raw_output:
EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words):
Your lab results (white blood cells, hemoglobin, platelets) are all normal, which is good. These tests, however, do not provide information about your skin.

The most important information is that you have a mole that is changing in size and color. Any mole that changes over time should be evaluated by a doctor, even if it doesn't hurt or bleed. This is because changes can sometimes be a sign of skin cancer. While many changing moles are harmless, a professional examination is the only way to be sure. Given your history of sun exposure, it is especially important to have this checked. A doctor will need to look at the mole to decide if it needs to be removed and tested (a biopsy).

TRIAGE:
B

CONFIDENCE:
95%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words):
Your lab results and vital signs are normal, which is reassuring. However, these tests do not give information about a specific spot on your skin. The most important detail is your description of a mole that is changing in size and color over several months. Any new or changing mole is a reason to see a doctor, regardless of skin tone. While you don't have alarming symptoms like pain or bleeding, the changes themselves warrant a professional evaluation. A doctor needs to physically examine the mole to determine if it is concerning and if a biopsy is needed to rule out skin cancer.

TRIAGE: <B>
- A: Fine to monitor at home
- B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
- C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
- D: Go to the ER now

CONFIDENCE: <95%>

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words):
Your lab results, which check your blood cells, are all normal. This is reassuring and suggests there is no sign of a general infection, anemia, or problems with your platelets.

However, the main concern is the mole on your shoulder that has been changing. Any mole that gets darker, larger, or changes in any way over time needs to be evaluated by a healthcare professional. While many changing moles are harmless, these changes can be an early sign of skin cancer, so it's very important to have it examined. The fact that it doesn't hurt or bleed is good, but the changes themselves are the key reason to get it checked.

TRIAGE: B

CONFIDENCE: 95%

The most important information you've provided is that the mole is changing in color and size. Any mole that changes, even slowly and without other symptoms like pain or bleeding, needs to be evaluated in person by a doctor. This is because change can be a warning sign for skin cancer. The main point of concern is the mole on your shoulder that has been changing in color and size.
